content.problemStatement:
Chef has NNN 6-sided standard dice. Each die has dimensions 1×1×11 \times 1 \times 11×1×1. Since Chef is bored during the quarantine, he decides to stack dice for fun.

First, Chef forms four vertical stacks of dice (not necessarily with the same height; empty stacks are allowed) on his table, which together make up a pile of dice with base area up to 2×22 \times 22×2. Among all such structures, the total visible surface area of Chef's structure must be the smallest possible.

Then, Chef calculates the number of [pips] (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pip_(counting)) on the visible faces of all dice in the structure. A face of a die is visible if it does not touch the table or another die.

Now, he is wondering: among all possible arrangements of dice, what is the maximum possible total number of visible pips? Since he is busy cooking, he is asking you to solve this.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T lines follow, each containing a single integer N – the number of dice.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the maximum possible total number of visible pips.

sampleTestCases.explanation: With a single die we hide the face with 1 pip (bottom). The other five faces show 2+3+4+5+6=20.

solution.approach: For the minimal visible surface area we must use a single vertical stack (all dice in one column) because the visible faces count equals 4N + k where k is the number of non‑empty stacks, and k is minimized to 1. In such a stack every die except the top has its top and bottom faces hidden – they are opposite faces, so the visible sum on each of those dice is 21‑7=14, independent of orientation. The top die has only its bottom face hidden; to maximise the sum we hide the smallest possible pip, i.e. 1, giving visible sum 21‑1=20. Hence total maximum pips = (N‑1)*14 + 20 = 14*N + 6. The formula works for all N and fits in 64‑bit signed integer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
